meta.title: Security Architecture: 7-Layer Defense in Depth
meta.excerpt: Complete guide to the seven-layer defense-in-depth security architecture protecting every request from ingress to response. Built on real implementations with OWASP mapping.

toc.title: Why This Is the Most Important Section

props.title: Why This Is the Most Important Section

props.text: In the modern web landscape, security isn't just a feature—it's the foundation that determines whether your application survives contact with the internet. This guide presents a battle-tested, defense-in-depth security architecture that protects every single request from ingress to response.

Every public web application faces: - **Bot attacks**: Credential stuffing, content scraping, form spam - **Script injection**: XSS attacks attempting to steal user data or sessions - **CSRF attacks**: Malicious sites forcing authenticated actions - **SQL injection**: Despite ORMs, user input can still reach database queries - **DDoS attempts**: Resource exhaustion through overwhelming traffic - **Data exfiltration**: Attackers probing for sensitive information leaks
**For CTOs**: This architecture maps directly to OWASP Top 10, demonstrating compliance with industry standards and reducing insurance premiums.
**For Developers**: Each layer is composable, testable, and doesn't require rewriting your application logic. Security becomes part of the framework, not bolt-on complexity.
**For Users**: They'll never see these layers working—and that's the point. Security should be invisible when it works, obvious only when it fails.

props.body: Complete walkthrough of each defense layer

props.text: Our security architecture follows the principle of **defense in depth**: multiple independent layers that each provide protection. If one layer fails or is bypassed, the others continue to protect.

1. **Rate Limiting**: Stops brute force attacks and bot floods at the gate 2. **CSRF Protection**: Validates that requests originate from your application 3. **Input Sanitization**: Strips dangerous HTML/scripts from user input 4. **Server Validation**: Type-safe validation of all incoming data 5. **Hydration Guards**: Prevents client-server mismatches from breaking security 6. **Environment Validation**: Ensures required secrets are present at runtime 7. **Safe Error Handling**: Never leaks sensitive information in error messages
These layers work in sequence:

props.code:
Client Request
    ↓
[1] Rate Limiting → Block if too many requests
    ↓
[2] CSRF Check → Reject if token missing/invalid
    ↓
[3] Sanitization → Strip dangerous content
    ↓
[4] Validation → Reject if data shape wrong
    ↓
[5] Hydration Guard → Ensure client-server sync
    ↓
[6] Environment Check → Verify runtime config
    ↓
[7] Error Handler → Safe error responses
    ↓
Server Action / API Route

props.text: Each layer operates independently. A bypass in one doesn't compromise the others.

props.body: Token bucket algorithm, IP tracking, configurable limits

props.text: Rate limiting is your first line of defense against abuse. It prevents: - Brute force login attempts - Form spam floods - API abuse and scraping - DDoS attacks (simple ones)
We use the **token bucket algorithm** for smooth, predictable rate limiting:

props.body: Double-submit cookie pattern with HMAC signing

props.text: CSRF (Cross-Site Request Forgery) attacks trick users into submitting forms on your site from a malicious third-party site. Our protection uses the **double-submit cookie pattern** with HMAC signing.

1. User logs into yoursite.com 2. User visits evilsite.com (in another tab) 3. evilsite.com contains a hidden form that POSTs to yoursite.com 4. Browser automatically includes yoursite.com cookies 5. Without CSRF protection, the request succeeds
Input sanitization removes dangerous HTML and JavaScript from user input before it reaches your database or is displayed to other users.

Even with React's built-in XSS protection, you need sanitization for: - Rich text editors (user-generated HTML) - Markdown rendering (can contain HTML) - Third-party API data (don't trust external sources) - Database migrations (existing data may be dirty)

props.body: Zod schemas with custom refinements per form type

props.text: After sanitization removes dangerous content, validation ensures the data has the correct shape, type, and business rules.

props.code:
// lib/validation/contact-schema.ts
import { z } from 'zod'

export const contactFormSchema = z.object({
  name: z.string()
    .min(2, 'Name must be at least 2 characters')
    .max(100, 'Name must be less than 100 characters')
    .regex(/^[a-zA-Z\s'-]+$/, 'Name contains invalid characters'),
  
  email: z.string()
    .email('Invalid email address')
    .min(5)
    .max(255)
    .refine(
      email => !email.endsWith('.test'),
      'Test email addresses are not allowed'
    ),
  
  phone: z.string()
    .optional()
    .refine(
      phone => !phone || /^[\d\s()+-]+$/.test(phone),
      'Phone contains invalid characters'
    ),
  
  message: z.string()
    .min(10, 'Message must be at least 10 characters')
    .max(5000, 'Message must be less than 5000 characters')
    .refine(
      msg => !/https?:\/\//i.test(msg),
      'Messages containing URLs are not allowed'
    ),
  
  interests: z.array(z.string())
    .min(1, 'Select at least one interest')
    .max(5, 'Select no more than 5 interests'),
})

export type ContactFormData = z.infer<typeof contactFormSchema>

props.body: Client-server mismatch detection and safe rendering

props.text: Hydration mismatches occur when the server renders one thing but the client expects another. This can break security assumptions if not handled properly.

- **CSRF tokens**: Must be consistent server → client - **User state**: Can't assume client has same data as server - **Feature flags**: Environment-based features must hydrate safely - **A/B tests**: Variant selection must be deterministic

props.body: Runtime env var checking and startup guards

props.text: Missing or invalid environment variables can break security features silently. Validate them at startup.

props.body: Never leak stack traces, structured error responses

props.text: Errors can leak sensitive information like database schemas, file paths, and internal logic. Always sanitize errors before sending to clients.

props.body: How each layer maps to OWASP threat categories

props.text: This architecture directly addresses the OWASP Top 10 web application security risks:

### A01: Broken Access Control - **Layer 2 (CSRF)**: Prevents unauthorized cross-origin requests - **Layer 4 (Validation)**: Ensures users can only submit valid operations - **Layer 6 (Environment)**: Validates RBAC configurations

### A02: Cryptographic Failures - **Layer 2 (CSRF)**: HMAC signing for token integrity - **Layer 6 (Environment)**: Validates presence of encryption keys - **Best Practice**: Use HTTPS everywhere (enforced at infrastructure level)

### A03: Injection - **Layer 3 (Sanitization)**: Removes SQL/HTML/script injection attempts - **Layer 4 (Validation)**: Type-safe schemas prevent malformed data - **Best Practice**: Use ORMs with parameterized queries
### A04: Insecure Design - **All Layers**: Defense in depth is secure design by definition - **Architecture**: Each layer is independently testable and auditable
### A05: Security Misconfiguration - **Layer 6 (Environment)**: Startup validation catches misconfigurations - **Layer 7 (Errors)**: Never exposes internal configuration in errors
### A06: Vulnerable Components - **Best Practice**: Regular dependency updates via Dependabot - **Layer 6 (Environment)**: Can enforce minimum versions

### A07: Identification and Authentication Failures - **Layer 1 (Rate Limiting)**: Prevents brute force attacks - **Layer 2 (CSRF)**: Prevents session hijacking attempts - **Best Practice**: Use NextAuth.js with secure session management

### A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures - **Layer 2 (CSRF)**: HMAC signing ensures request integrity - **Layer 4 (Validation)**: Zod schemas validate data integrity - **Best Practice**: Use SRI for third-party scripts

### A09: Security Logging and Monitoring Failures - **Layer 7 (Errors)**: Structured error logging to Sentry - **Layer 1 (Rate Limiting)**: Log suspicious patterns - **Best Practice**: Monitor rate limit violations, validation failures

### A10: Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) - **Layer 3 (Sanitization)**: Strips URLs from user input where appropriate - **Layer 4 (Validation)**: Validates allowed URL patterns - **Best Practice**: Whitelist external API endpoints
✅ **7/10** directly addressed by architecture layers ✅ **3/10** covered by best practices and infrastructure ✅ **100%** OWASP Top 10 coverage
This makes the architecture **audit-ready** for security compliance reviews.
